findings.title: EA is transforming from static documentation to real-time AI-augmented governance
findings.description: Multiple sources converge on a fundamental shift in enterprise architecture practice. EA is moving away from periodic reviews and manually maintained documentation toward continuously adaptive, AI-augmented governance. This transformation affects not just tools but the role of the architect itself — evolving into AI governance strategists, knowledge curators, and orchestrators of human-AI collaboration. While the direction is clear and broadly supported, evidence of completed transformations at scale remains limited.

claims.statement: Enterprise architecture is undergoing a fundamental transformation driven by AI, shifting from static documentation and periodic review toward real-time, AI-augmented, and continuously adaptive governance.

findings.title: AI governance must be embedded, adaptive, and lifecycle-wide
findings.description: The strongest consensus across sources is that governance cannot be an afterthought. Controls — runtime validation, intent logging, identity authentication, access controls — must be embedded throughout the AI agent lifecycle. Critically, governance frameworks must adapt to varying degrees of AI autonomy rather than applying uniform controls. Four in ten organizations report their governance is insufficient, yet regulatory requirements (EU AI Act, data privacy laws) are accelerating. Organizations that treat governance as a competitive advantage rather than just a compliance burden will be better positioned.

findings.title: Human oversight remains essential despite increasing AI autonomy
findings.description: Even as AI takes on more autonomous capabilities, sources consistently emphasize that human judgment cannot be replaced for architectural trade-offs, strategic prioritization, and stakeholder negotiations. Agentic AI exhibits a dual nature as both tool and worker, creating tension between efficiency gains and the need for meaningful human oversight. Organizations must implement deliberate human-in-the-loop mechanisms rather than assuming AI can operate unsupervised.

findings.description: Agentic AI systems — autonomous agents capable of reasoning, planning, and executing across enterprise functions — require fundamentally new architectural patterns.
